sections.heading: Surgery
sections.content:
Surgery is the primary treatment option for patients with localized gastric cancer with the goal of achieving a complete resection with negative margins (R0 resection).

Principles:
- The primary goal is R0 resection; however, only 50% of patients will achieve this.
- The type of resection (subtotal vs. total gastrectomy) and extent of lymph node dissection (D1 vs. D2) are key considerations.
- Adequate gastric resection with lymphadenectomy is standard for T1b–T3 tumors. T4b tumors require en bloc resection of involved structures.
- Gastric resection should include regional lymphatics with a goal of examining ≥16 lymph nodes (retrieval and assessment of >30 nodes is desirable).
- Routine splenectomy is not indicated unless the spleen is involved or extensive hilar adenopathy is noted.
- Surgery for palliation should be reserved for symptoms (obstruction, uncontrollable bleeding) when nonsurgical options are not feasible.

Resectability criteria: Gastric adenocarcinoma is considered unresectable for cure if there is evidence of: 1) Locoregionally advanced disease: infiltration of the root of the mesentery or para-aortic lymph nodes highly suspicious on imaging or confirmed by biopsy; invasion or encasement of major vascular structures (excluding the splenic vessels); 2) Distant metastasis or peritoneal seeding (including positive peritoneal cytology, which is defined as pM1 disease).

Pre-operative optimisation: Consider placing a feeding tube in select patients undergoing total gastrectomy, especially if postoperative chemoradiation appears likely. Nutritional assessment and optimization are important. For patients undergoing non-operative management (e.g., immunotherapy for MSI-H/dMMR), gastrectomy with appropriate lymphadenectomy remains the standard outside of prospective organ-preservation clinical trials.

cards.name: Procedure
cards.detail:
Name: Distal Gastrectomy
Approach: Open, laparoscopic, or robotic
Indicated for: Tumors in the distal two-thirds of the stomach.
Key complications: Anastomotic leak, dumping syndrome, nutritional deficiencies.
Operative mortality: Variable, generally <5% in high-volume centers.

cards.name: Procedure
cards.detail:
Name: Subtotal Gastrectomy
Approach: Open, laparoscopic, or robotic
Indicated for: Selected distal gastric cancers. Has similar outcomes to total gastrectomy for distal cancers with fewer complications.
Key complications: Similar to distal gastrectomy.
Operative mortality: Similar to distal gastrectomy.

cards.name: Procedure
cards.detail:
Name: Total Gastrectomy
Approach: Open, laparoscopic, or robotic
Indicated for: Proximal gastric cancers, diffuse-type cancer, or when adequate margins require it.
Key complications: Anastomotic leak, dumping syndrome, severe nutritional deficiencies (B12, iron, etc.), weight loss.
Operative mortality: Higher than subtotal gastrectomy.

cards.name: Procedure
cards.detail:
Name: Endoscopic Resection (EMR or ESD)
Approach: Endoscopic
Indicated for: Selected early-stage (T1a or superficial T1b ≤500 µm) gastric cancers meeting specific criteria (≤2 cm, well/moderately differentiated, no LVI, negative margins).
Key complications: Bleeding, perforation.
Operative mortality: Very low.

cards.name: Procedure
cards.detail:
Name: Cytoreductive Surgery (CRS) with HIPEC
Approach: Open surgery followed by hyperthermic intraperitoneal chemotherapy
Indicated for: Carefully selected patients with peritoneal carcinoma as only disease, low PCI (≤10), and potential for complete cytoreduction.
Key complications: Bowel anastomotic leak, intra-abdominal abscess, renal dysfunction.
Operative mortality: Variable, reported as 0% in some series.
